items.q: Why does historical context matter when reading literature?
items.correct: Texts emerge from and respond to specific historical conditions — context illuminates references, assumptions and arguments that might otherwise be invisible.
items.misconception: Context just means writing about what the author had for breakfast.
items.random_wrong: Context is required because all texts are about history.
items.why: Students treat context as biographical trivia. Useful context is what the text engages with — politics, ideology, genre conventions — not personal detail of the author's life.

items.correct: A novel tracing a protagonist's moral and psychological development from youth to adulthood — typically involving formative experiences and a clearer sense of self.
items.misconception: Any novel with a young protagonist is a bildungsroman.
items.random_wrong: A bildungsroman is a novel about constructing buildings.
items.why: The focus must be on inward growth and development — having a young protagonist isn't enough. Students apply the term loosely to any story featuring a child or teenager.

items.q: What is the dramatic function of a soliloquy?
items.correct: It reveals a character's inner thoughts directly to the audience while the character is alone on stage — a convention granting access to private psychology.
items.misconception: A soliloquy is any long speech a character gives.
items.random_wrong: A soliloquy is when characters argue with each other.
items.why: Students confuse soliloquy (alone, inner thoughts) with monologue (long speech, possibly to others). The key feature is solitude and direct access to thought — losing this loses the convention's force.

items.q: Who wrote Tradition and the Individual Talent?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: T S Eliot. “F R Leavis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Creative misreading. “Direct quotation” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Tradition and the Individual Talent published?
items.why: The real figure is 1919. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
